text.title: Interview Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Concrete insight examples from interview batches and four antipatterns that invalidate interview data.

section.title: What Good Interview Output Looks Like

example.note: After 10 problem interviews on a productivity tool: 8/10 mentioned the same frustration (manual status updates). Average time wasted: 3 hours per week. Willingness to pay: $50–100/month. Verbatim: "I would kill for a solution to this." These four signals together (convergence, time cost, WTP, emotional intensity) justify moving to solution validation.

Customer interview insight cluster: 8/10 mentioned same frustration; verbatim "I would pay $100 to not deal with this"; behavior — currently using 3 tools as workaround; insight — strong problem-solution fit indicated by active workaround behavior (workarounds are the most reliable signal that a real job exists).

antipattern.description: Leading questions: "Don't you think X is great?" or "Isn't it frustrating when Y?"
antipattern.reason: Leading questions prime the answer; the user confirms your hypothesis rather than revealing their reality. All signal from a leading question must be discarded.

antipattern.description: Future hypotheticals: "Would you use this if it existed?" or "Would you pay for this?"
antipattern.reason: Stated future intent does not predict behavior. People are optimistic about their future selves. Only past behavior and present commitments (pre-orders, sign-ups with a cost) are reliable.

antipattern.description: Pitching during the interview: explaining your solution before the pain has been fully explored.
antipattern.reason: Once the researcher pitches, the user evaluates the solution instead of describing their problem. The remainder of the interview produces solution feedback, not problem validation.

antipattern.description: Asking multiple questions at once: "What is the most frustrating part and how much time does it take and would you pay for a fix?"
antipattern.reason: The user answers only the last or easiest question. Each question must land and breathe independently.

rule.statement: LLMs can draft guides and synthesize transcripts but cannot conduct live interviews; scrub PII before feeding transcripts to any LLM; validate synthesized insight clusters against raw source before using for decisions.
rule.rationale: Transcript synthesis misses sarcasm, hesitation, and emotional subtext; confirmation bias compounds when the agent was given a one-sided brief.

text.reference: LLM prompt to draft an interview guide from a hypothesis
LLM prompt to extract themes and surprising findings from transcripts
Batch keyword counter across transcript .txt files
